sections.heading: PART II Statement of Policy
sections.summary: This section lists the subsections of Part II, which outlines the policy framework for cybersecurity in the banking sector. It specifies the purpose, scope, responsibility allocation, and examples of cyber risk sources that the banking sector must address to ensure robust cybersecurity practices.

requirements.text: 2.1 Purpose
2.2 Scope
2.3 Responsibility
2.4 Examples of Sources of Cyber Risk

sections.heading: PART III Specific Requirements
sections.summary: This section mandates that banking institutions' boards must actively oversee cyber risk management by ensuring ethical governance, strategic alignment, adequate budgeting, and regular reviews of cybersecurity frameworks. Senior management must implement these strategies, identify critical assets, establish incident response procedures, and maintain continuous monitoring to mitigate cyber threats while aligning with business continuity requirements. This section mandates banking institutions in Kenya to implement robust cybersecurity measures, including strong authentication protocols, skilled staff with enhanced background checks, regular cyber risk assessments, incident response planning, and quarterly reporting to the board on cybersecurity posture. It requires the establishment of a Chief Information Security Officer (CISO) with specific responsibilities such as overseeing cybersecurity programs, maintaining asset inventories, conducting risk assessments, and ensuring timely incident response. Institutions must also collaborate with security agencies to share cyber threat intelligence and maintain comprehensive incident response frameworks. Financial institutions must implement
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes foundational governance obligations for cybersecurity that directly impact data transfer assessments by requiring institutions to maintain robust oversight mechanisms, risk ownership structures, and continuous monitoring of cyber threats before transferring or processing data across borders. This section directly impacts Data Transfer Impact Assessments by requiring institutions to establish robust cybersecurity frameworks that protect customer data during transfers and processing, including incident response planning and third-party risk management for data handling a

requirements.text: Board of Directors must oversee cultivation of ethical governance culture and security risk awareness
Board must engage management in establishing cybersecurity vision, risk appetite, and strategic direction
Board must allocate adequate cybersecurity budget based on institution structure
Board must review management's determination of cybersecurity preparedness alignment with cyber risks
Board must establish or review cybersecurity risk ownership and management accountability
Board must approve and continuously review cybersecurity strategy, governance charter, policy, and framework
Board must ensure cybersecurity policy applies to all operating entities including subsidiaries and geographic regions
Board must review implementation of cybersecurity framework at least once every 12 months
Board must incorporate cybersecurity as standard agenda in meetings
Board must review results of management's ongoing monitoring of cyber threats
Board must ensure cybersecurity policy includes monitoring metrics and trend analysis
Oversee deployment of strong authentication measures to protect customer data, transactions and systems
Ensure provision of sufficient number of skilled staff for cybersecurity management with enhanced background and competency checks
Ensure timely and regular reporting to the board on cyber risk status
Establish a cybersecurity benchmarking framework with Board's endorsement
Incorporate cybersecurity as a standard agenda in Senior Management meetings
Document cybersecurity incident response plan addressing roles, incident detection, reporting, and escalation strategies
Create a post-incident analysis framework for corrective actions
Oversee evaluation and management of risks introduced by third-party service providers with attestation/assurance reports from independent auditors
CISO must oversee and implement cybersecurity program and enforce cybersecurity policy
CISO must ensure current enterprise-wide knowledge base of users, devices, applications and their relationships
CISO must ensure information systems meet institutional needs and comply with business strategies and risk policies
CISO must design cybersecurity controls considering internal and external users
CISO must organize professional cyber-related trainings for staff
CISO must ensure regular and comprehensive cyber risk assessments
CISO must ensure processes for timely monitoring of IT systems to detect cybersecurity events
CISO must report to CEO quarterly on confidentiality, integrity, availability of information systems, policy exceptions, program effectiveness, and material cybersecurity events
CISO must ensure timely updates to incident response mechanism and Business Continuity Plan based on cyber threat intelligence
CISO must incorporate scenario analysis for material cyber-attacks and control gaps
CISO must ensure frequent data backups of critical IT systems to separate storage locations
CISO must ensure clear roles and responsibilities for cyber risk management in emergencies
CISO must continuously test disaster recovery and Business Continuity Plans
Institutions must engage external consultants with cybersecurity expertise for annual independent cyber threat tests
Institutions must incorporate qualified ICT auditors within Internal Audit team
Internal ICT auditors must continuously review and report on cyber risks and controls
Internal ICT auditors must assess design and effectiveness of cybersecurity framework
Internal ICT auditors must conduct regular independent threat and vulnerability assessments
Internal ICT auditors must report to the board on assessment findings
Internal ICT auditors must conduct comprehensive penetration tests
Risk management function must assess cybersecurity risks against institutional risk appetite
Risk management function must monitor current and emerging cybersecurity risks and regulatory changes
Risk management function must collaborate with system administrators to ensure appropriate control design
Risk management function must maintain comprehensive cyber risk registers
Risk management function must ensure implementation of cyber risk management strategy
Risk management function must safeguard confidentiality, integrity and availability of information
Risk management function must establish and maintain IT asset inventory by business criticality
Risk management function must quantify potential impact of residual cyber risks
Risk management function must report all enterprise risks consistently to the board
Risk management function must conduct red team exercises
External auditors must ensure IT audit scope includes understanding of IT infrastructure and operations
External auditors must understand extent of automated controls affecting financial reporting
External auditors must conduct independent threat and vulnerability assessments
External auditors must review approved cybersecurity strategy and policy
External auditors must conduct comprehensive penetration tests
External auditors must report annually to the board and CBK on assessment findings
Institutions must have adequate governance of outsourcing agreements including due diligence on service providers
Institutions must consider outsourcing agreements as critical infrastructure for regulation and protection
Institutions must select vendors based on compliance and risk assessments
Institutions must ensure computing resources are secured including registrations, licensing, compliance and verification
Institutions must ensure outsourcing contracts require service providers to comply with legal and regulatory frameworks
Institutions must understand inherent risks from each third party
Institutions must perform analytics on outsourcing portfolio to identify relative risks
Institutions must work collaboratively with third parties to mitigate risks
Institutions must monitor contracted third parties for changes in business and cyber posture
Institutions must develop exit management strategies and contingency plans
Institutions should implement IT security awareness training programmes to provide information on good IT security practices, common threat types and the institution's policies and procedures.
The training should be provided to all employees including senior management and the board.
A formalized plan should be put in place to provide ongoing technical training to cybersecurity specialists within the institution.

Cybersecurity awareness and information should be provided to the institution's customers, clients, suppliers, partners, outsourced service providers and other third parties who have links to the bank's IT infrastructure.

sections.source_quote: Board of Directors must oversee cultivation and promotion of an ethical governance, management culture and awareness. Setting 'the right tone from the top' is a crucial element in fostering a robust cyber risk management culture.
